skill.name: startup-business-analyst-market-opportunity
skill.description: Generate market opportunity analysis with TAM, SAM, SOM sizing, source-backed assumptions, and investor-ready reporting.
skill.summary: Create structured startup market sizing reports with bottom-up and top-down validation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives caused by market-sizing terms, markdown backticks, and TAM/SAM/SOM references. The real concern is broad declared tool access, including Bash, Write, Edit, WebSearch, and WebFetch, for a research workflow with no clear need for shell execution.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Tool Permissions
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash, Write, Edit, WebSearch, and WebFetch access. The workflow requires web research and optional report writing, but Bash and Edit are not justified by the instructions. This increases the impact of prompt injection or misuse.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool declaration is explicit and the instructions show only research and optional file output needs. No malicious behavior is present, but unnecessary Bash access is a meaningful permission risk.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SAM Database Detection
low_findings.description: The static scanner interpreted SAM as a Windows SAM database reference. In this file, SAM means Serviceable Available Market within TAM/SAM/SOM market sizing, so these detections are not credential or system database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text consistently discusses market sizing and Serviceable Available Market calculations. No evidence of Windows credential database access was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections are markdown inline code and fenced examples, including a resource path, a formula, a filename, and example command names. They are not executable shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is markdown formatting and example text, not code passed to an interpreter. No shell invocation or user input interpolation appears in the file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Cryptography and Reconnaissance Terms
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and network reconnaissance detections appear to come from ordinary business-analysis language and acronyms. No cryptographic algorithm, port scan, host discovery, or reconnaissance command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains market opportunity instructions, not crypto or network tooling. The specific lines are descriptive business text without executable commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Tool Access in Community Skill
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requests shell, filesystem editing, and network tools. These tools can be legitimate for research workflows, but the permission set is broader than necessary for the documented behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The declaration is explicit and broad. Risk depends on runtime enforcement and user prompts, so this is not evidence of malicious intent by itself.

content.value_statement: Market sizing is hard when assumptions, data sources, and investor expectations are scattered. This skill structures TAM, SAM, SOM, validation, and reporting into a repeatable workflow.
actual_capabilities: Guides context gathering for product, customer, pricing, stage, and geography.
Builds bottom-up TAM calculations for SaaS, consumer, marketplace, and transaction models.
Applies geographic, product fit, and readiness filters to estimate SAM.
Creates conservative Year 3 and Year 5 SOM projections.
Prompts web research with cited sources and publication dates.
Produces a structured markdown market sizing report.
limitations: Results depend on available market data and source quality.
It does not replace investor diligence or expert market research.
The user must provide clear pricing, segment, and geography assumptions.
Forecasts can be inaccurate if adoption assumptions are too optimistic.

use_cases.title: Validate a Fundraising Narrative
use_cases.description: Build a sourced TAM, SAM, and SOM story before preparing investor materials.

use_cases.title: Compare Expansion Segments
use_cases.description: Estimate which customer segment or region has the strongest near-term serviceable market.

use_cases.title: Review Market Claims
use_cases.description: Pressure test a market-size claim using bottom-up math and top-down validation.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Market Size
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me size the market for a startup. Ask me for the product, customer, pricing, stage, and geography before calculating TAM, SAM, and SOM.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a SaaS Opportunity
prompt_templates.scenario: B2B software opportunity with known pricing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a market opportunity analysis for a B2B SaaS product. Use bottom-up TAM by segment, validate with public market data, then estimate SAM and SOM.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Markets
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the market opportunity for these two customer segments. Show TAM, SAM, SOM, assumptions, source quality, competitive intensity, and recommended priority.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Investor Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced report for fundraising or diligence.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare an investor-ready market opportunity report with executive summary, market definition, bottom-up analysis, top-down validation, SAM filters, SOM projection, growth drivers, risks, and next steps.

output_examples.input: AI email marketing software for North American e-commerce companies with more than one million dollars in revenue.
output_examples.output: A report outline with customer segments, estimated company counts, ACV assumptions, TAM calculation, SAM filters, five-year SOM, and cited data gaps.

output_examples.input: Compare healthcare clinics and independent dental practices for a scheduling automation product.
output: A comparison of adoption readiness and competitive intensity.
A recommendation for the stronger initial beachhead.

output_examples.input: Pressure test a claim that a startup can reach 12 percent of SAM in five years.
output_examples.output: A conservative review of market share assumptions, required customer count, go-to-market capacity, public benchmarks, and risks to credibility.

best_practices: Start with bottom-up sizing and use top-down research as validation.
Document every assumption, source, date, and calculation step.
Keep SOM conservative unless clear evidence supports a higher share.
anti_patterns: Do not rely only on large market reports without segment math.
Do not cherry-pick optimistic sources while ignoring contrary data.
Do not present market share above 10 percent without strong evidence.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces a structured market opportunity analysis with TAM, SAM, SOM, assumptions, validation, risks, and next steps.

faq.question: Does it research current market data?
faq.answer: Yes, it prompts web research and source citation when the assistant has search or fetch tools available.

faq.question: Can it handle different business models?
faq.answer: Yes, it includes approaches for SaaS, consumer, marketplace, transaction, and e-commerce models.

faq.question: Is the output investor ready?
faq.answer: It creates a strong draft, but users should verify sources, assumptions, and financial implications before sharing it.

faq.question: What inputs improve the analysis?
faq.answer: Clear customer segments, geography, pricing, adoption constraints, and competitor context improve the result.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill appears legitimate, but it declares broad tool permissions, including Bash and file editing, beyond the core workflow.
